---
title: "Chapter 42 — 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 41 · 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics](ch41.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [43 · 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference →](ch43.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Training a model is the easy part; keeping it useful in production for months is the hard part. **MLOps** is the discipline of building, deploying, monitoring, and continuously improving machine learning systems with the same rigor that DevOps brought to software — and **LLMOps** is the variant that copes with the peculiarities of large language models (prompts, tokens, nondeterminism, eval-by-judgment). This chapter sits at the production end of the field: it assumes you already know how to train a model and asks how you operate it reliably and affordably at scale.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Production & operations layer of AI/ML · used to ship, version, monitor, and retrain models (and LLM apps) safely · key idea: a model is a living system whose data and behavior drift, so treat the whole lifecycle — not just the artifact — as the thing you engineer.
:::

## 42.1 — The ML lifecycle and MLOps maturity

The mistake that defines early ML projects is treating the trained model as the finish line. In reality the model is one node in a loop: you collect data, train, validate, deploy, watch it in the wild, discover it has gone stale, and feed what you learned back into the next training run. **MLOps** is the set of practices and automation that makes that loop repeatable instead of heroic.

A useful way to gauge where a team stands is the **maturity ladder**, popularized by Google's MLOps levels. At the bottom everything is manual; at the top the loop runs itself.

- **Level 0 — manual:** a data scientist trains in a notebook, hands a pickle file to an engineer, who wraps it in an API. Works once. Nobody can reproduce it, and retraining is a person remembering to do it.
- **Level 1 — ML pipeline automation:** the training process is a parameterized, version-controlled pipeline. Retraining on fresh data is one command, and **continuous training (CT)** can be triggered on a schedule or by a drift alarm.
- **Level 2 — CI/CD automation:** the pipeline *itself* is built, tested, and deployed automatically. A change to feature code or model architecture flows through automated tests and into production with human approval, not human labor.

The reason this ladder matters is that each rung removes a class of 3am page. Manual deploys fail silently; automated pipelines fail loudly in CI where it's cheap to fix. Notice the new vocabulary too: classic DevOps has **CI** (continuous integration) and **CD** (continuous deployment); MLOps adds a third **CT** (continuous training), because in ML the *data* changes even when nobody touches the code, so the model itself needs a refresh cycle that pure software never had.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  D[Data] --> T[Train]
  T --> V[Validate]
  V --> R[Registry]
  R --> S[Serve]
  S --> M[Monitor]
  M -->|drift / decay| D
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Don't chase Level 2 on day one. The lazy, correct order is: make it reproducible (Level 1) before you make it automatic (Level 2). Automating a pipeline nobody can reproduce just lets you ship the same mistake faster.
:::

## 42.2 — Experiment tracking and reproducibility

A research phase produces dozens of runs that differ in learning rate, architecture, data slice, and random seed. Six weeks later someone asks "which run produced the model we shipped, and can you rebuild it?" Without tracking, the honest answer is no. **Experiment tracking** records, for every run, the code version, hyperparameters, dataset version, environment, and resulting metrics, so any result can be found and reconstructed.

Tools like **MLflow** and **Weights & Biases (W&B)** give you a logging API and a dashboard that turns this into a searchable table of runs. The mechanism is simple — you log a dictionary of params and a stream of metrics keyed to a run ID:

```python
# the essence of an experiment tracker (what MLflow/W&B do under the hood)
import json, time, uuid

def start_run(params):
    run_id = uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]
    record = {"id": run_id, "params": params, "metrics": [], "ts": time.time()}
    return record

def log_metric(record, name, value, step):
    record["metrics"].append({"name": name, "value": value, "step": step})

def end_run(record):
    with open(f"runs/{record['id']}.json", "w") as f:
        json.dump(record, f)        # now searchable, comparable, reproducible

run = start_run({"lr": 3e-4, "seed": 42})
log_metric(run, "val_acc", 0.91, step=10)
end_run(run)
```

Tracking metrics is necessary but not sufficient for **reproducibility** — the ability to get the same result twice. Three things bite people:

- **Seeds:** neural net training, data shuffling, and weight init all draw from random number generators. Set and log every seed (Python, NumPy, the framework, and the data loader workers). Note that GPU kernel nondeterminism can still cause tiny drift unless you force deterministic ops.
- **Environment pinning:** "works on my machine" is a dependency version mismatch. Pin exact versions (a lockfile, a container image digest) so the run uses the same library builds tomorrow.
- **Data versioning:** the same code on different data is a different experiment. The dataset must be versioned too (next section).

::: {.callout-warning}
Logging the final accuracy but not the seed, the data snapshot, and the exact library versions is the most common reproducibility failure. A number you can't regenerate is a rumor, not a result.
:::

## 42.3 — Versioning models and data, and lineage

Git versions your code beautifully and your 5 GB training set terribly — large binaries bloat the repo and diffs are meaningless. The fix is to version data and models the way Git versions code, but storing the *content* in object storage and keeping only a lightweight pointer in Git.

**DVC (Data Version Control)** does exactly this: it hashes a large file or directory, uploads the content to a remote (S3, GCS), and commits a tiny `.dvc` file holding the hash. Checking out an old commit and running `dvc checkout` brings back the exact data that commit pointed to. The trick is just **content-addressing** — naming a blob by the hash of its bytes, so identical content always gets the same name and any change produces a new one:

```python
import hashlib

def content_hash(path):
    h = hashlib.sha256()
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(8192), b""):
            h.update(chunk)
    return h.hexdigest()          # this hash is the pointer Git stores

# data_v1.csv -> "a3f9..." ; change one row -> entirely different hash
```

A **model registry** (MLflow Registry, SageMaker, Vertex) is the analogous store for trained models. It holds versioned model artifacts plus their stage (`Staging`, `Production`, `Archived`), so "promote model v7 to production" and "roll back to v6" are explicit, audited actions rather than someone re-copying a file.

Tie all of this together and you get **lineage**: a traceable chain from a prediction back to the model version, the training run, the data snapshot, and the code commit that produced it. Lineage is what lets you answer "why did the model do that?" and "what else is affected?" after an incident — and it's increasingly a regulatory requirement, not just a nicety.

| Asset | Versioned with | What the pointer is |
|---|---|---|
| Code | Git | commit SHA |
| Data | DVC / lakeFS | content hash in `.dvc` |
| Model | Model registry | version + stage tag |
| Experiment | MLflow / W&B | run ID |

## 42.4 — Pipeline orchestration

Once training is more than one script, you have a **pipeline**: ingest → validate → featurize → train → evaluate → register. These steps have dependencies (you can't train before features exist), some can run in parallel, any can fail, and you want retries, scheduling, and a visual record of what ran. An **orchestrator** turns this into a managed **DAG** (directed acyclic graph) of tasks — "directed" because each arrow points one way, "acyclic" because the steps never loop back on themselves, which is exactly what lets the tool compute a safe execution order.

The dominant tools differ mostly in philosophy:

- **Airflow** — the veteran; schedule-driven DAGs defined in Python. Huge ecosystem, but historically awkward at passing data between tasks.
- **Kubeflow Pipelines** — Kubernetes-native, built for ML specifically; each step is a container, which makes heavy GPU training steps natural.
- **Prefect** and **Dagster** — modern, Python-first; Dagster emphasizes data assets and typing, Prefect emphasizes dynamic flows and a gentle developer experience.

The shared mental model is the DAG. You declare tasks and their dependencies; the orchestrator figures out order, parallelism, and what to re-run after a failure.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  I[ingest] --> Vd[validate]
  Vd --> F[featurize]
  F --> Tr[train]
  Tr --> Ev[evaluate]
  Ev --> Rg[register]
```

The pitfall worth naming: orchestrators are for **batch workflows**, not for serving live requests. Don't wire user-facing inference through Airflow because the DAG was already there — that couples your request latency to a scheduler built for nightly jobs.

## 42.5 — Feature stores and training-serving skew

Here is a subtle, expensive bug. In training you compute "average purchase over the last 30 days" with a tidy pandas groupby over historical data. In production you re-implement the same feature in Java inside a low-latency service. The two implementations disagree — different time-window handling, a different null policy — so the model sees features at serving time that don't match what it learned from. This is **training-serving skew**, and it silently degrades a model that looked perfect offline.

A **feature store** (Feast, Tecton, and the cloud-native ones) attacks the root cause: compute each feature *once*, then serve the same values to both training and inference. It has two faces over one definition:

- an **offline store** (a warehouse) that produces large historical feature tables for training, and
- an **online store** (a low-latency KV store like Redis) that serves the latest feature values to the live model.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
  Def[feature definition] --> Off[(offline store / warehouse)]
  Def --> On[(online store / Redis)]
  Off --> Train[training]
  On --> Serve[online inference]
```

A second job of the offline store is **point-in-time correctness**: when building a training row labeled at time $t$, you must join the feature values *as they were at $t$*, never a value computed later. Leaking future information inflates offline metrics and then collapses in production. Formally, for a training event at time $t$ you want the feature

$$ f(t) = g\big(\{x_s : s \le t\}\big), $$

a function only of data available up to $t$. Feature stores enforce this join so you don't hand-roll it (and get it wrong) per project.

::: {.callout-warning}
A feature store is real infrastructure to run and is overkill for a single batch model. Reach for one when multiple models or teams share features, or when online/offline skew has actually bitten you — not by default.
:::

## 42.6 — Deployment strategies and monitoring

You have a new model that beats the old one offline. Offline metrics lie often enough that you should never flip 100% of traffic to it at once. **Progressive delivery** lets you expose a new model to a controlled slice of reality and watch before committing.

| Strategy | What it does | Bought with it |
|---|---|---|
| **Shadow** | new model scores live traffic; outputs logged, not served | risk-free real-world test, no user impact |
| **Canary** | a small % of traffic gets the new model | early blast-radius limit |
| **Blue-green** | two full environments; flip traffic, instant rollback | fast, clean reversion |
| **A/B test** | split traffic, compare a business metric statistically | causal evidence the new model is *better*, not just different |

The distinction that trips people: **shadow** answers "does it crash or produce garbage?"; an **A/B test** answers "does it lift the metric we care about?". Shadow is an engineering check, A/B is a product decision — you usually want both, in that order.

Deployment is not the end; it's the start of **monitoring**. A deployed model decays because the world it predicts over keeps moving, in two distinct ways you must not conflate:

- **Data drift (covariate shift):** the input distribution changes, $P(x)$ moves, while the relationship $P(y \mid x)$ stays the same. A new user segment with different feature values arrives. The model may still be correct, but it's now extrapolating.
- **Concept drift:** the relationship itself changes, $P(y \mid x)$ moves. Fraud tactics evolve, so the same transaction features now mean something different. This is the dangerous one — accuracy falls even though inputs look familiar.

You detect data drift cheaply without labels by comparing the live feature distribution to a training-time reference; concept drift usually needs labels (or a proxy) because it's about correctness, not inputs. A simple population-stability check on one feature:

```python
import numpy as np

def psi(reference, live, bins=10):
    # Population Stability Index: how far has this feature's distribution moved?
    edges = np.histogram_bin_edges(reference, bins=bins)
    r, _ = np.histogram(reference, edges); r = r / r.sum() + 1e-6
    l, _ = np.histogram(live, edges);      l = l / l.sum() + 1e-6
    return np.sum((l - r) * np.log(l / r))   # >0.2 ~ meaningful drift, investigate

# psi small -> stable; psi large -> input world has shifted, model may be stale
```

The other half of monitoring is the unglamorous **operational** layer that any production service needs: latency (p50/p95/p99), throughput, error rate, and saturation of CPU/GPU/memory. A model can be statistically healthy and still be failing users because the p99 latency blew past the timeout — so watch both the *prediction quality* signals (drift) and the *systems* signals (the classic SRE golden signals) on the same dashboard.

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb for which drift you have: if inputs look new but the model is still right where you can check, it's data drift — maybe just monitor. If inputs look normal but accuracy is sliding, it's concept drift — retrain now.
:::

## 42.7 — LLMOps: operating language-model applications

LLMOps inherits everything above and then breaks several of its assumptions. The model is often a third-party API you can't retrain; the "code" includes a **prompt** written in English; outputs are nondeterministic and free-form, so there's no single accuracy number; and every call costs money measured in **tokens** (the sub-word chunks a model reads and writes — see Chapter 30). The operational surface shifts accordingly.

**Prompt and config versioning.** The prompt is now production logic, so it belongs in version control with the same discipline as code — a prompt change can swing behavior as hard as a model swap. Treat the prompt, the model id, temperature, and tool definitions as a versioned bundle, and you can A/B and roll back prompts exactly like models.

**Eval-in-CI.** You can't unit-test a free-form generation with `assertEqual`. Instead you keep an **evaluation set** of inputs with graded criteria and run it in CI on every prompt or model change, scoring with a mix of programmatic checks (does the JSON parse? is the cited document present?) and an **LLM-as-judge** — a second model prompted with a rubric to grade the first model's answer. The gate is statistical — average score must not regress — not exact-match.

**Cost and token tracking.** Spend scales with tokens, so it must be a first-class metric, attributed per feature and per user to catch a runaway loop before the invoice does.

```python
# token-cost accounting, the unit every LLMOps dashboard is built on
PRICE = {"in": 3.0/1e6, "out": 15.0/1e6}   # $ per token (example rates)

def call_cost(in_tokens, out_tokens):
    return in_tokens * PRICE["in"] + out_tokens * PRICE["out"]

# 1200 prompt + 400 completion tokens
print(round(call_cost(1200, 400), 5))   # -> 0.0096  ($/call; multiply by QPS)
```

**Semantic caching.** Exact-match caching rarely hits because users phrase the same question differently. A **semantic cache** instead embeds the query, looks for a stored query within a cosine-similarity threshold, and returns the cached answer on a hit — cutting both latency and cost for near-duplicate questions.

**Tracing and observability.** A modern LLM app is a chain — retrieve, plan, call tools, generate — so when an answer is wrong you need the whole trace, not just the final string. **Tracing** (LangSmith, Langfuse, OpenTelemetry-based tools) records each step's inputs, outputs, latency, and tokens, turning "the bot said something dumb" into a debuggable span tree.

**Guardrails.** Because inputs and outputs are open-ended, you add an input/output safety layer: block prompt-injection and PII on the way in, validate format and filter unsafe or off-topic content on the way out, before anything reaches the user.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  U[user] --> G1[input guardrail]
  G1 --> C[semantic cache]
  C -->|miss| L[LLM + tools]
  C -->|hit| Out
  L --> G2[output guardrail]
  G2 --> Out[response]
  L -.trace+tokens.-> Obs[(observability)]
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Shipping an LLM feature with no eval set and no tracing is the LLMOps equivalent of deploying without tests or logs. A prompt tweak that "looks better" on three hand-picked examples routinely regresses ten others you didn't check.
:::

## 42.8 — How MLOps and LLMOps differ

It helps to lay the two side by side, because LLMOps is not a rename of MLOps — it keeps the lifecycle skeleton but swaps out what you version, how you evaluate, and where the cost lives. The classic ML world centers on a model you trained and a number you can compute; the LLM world centers on a model you mostly *call* and an output you have to *judge*.

| Concern | Classic MLOps | LLMOps |
|---|---|---|
| Core artifact | model weights you trained | prompt + model id + config (often a 3rd-party model) |
| "Code" that drives behavior | feature + training code | the prompt, in natural language |
| Evaluation | a metric (accuracy, AUC) on a labeled set | graded rubric + LLM-as-judge; statistical, not exact |
| Improvement lever | retrain on fresh data | edit prompt, swap model, add retrieval/fine-tune |
| Dominant runtime cost | training compute | per-token inference cost at serving |
| Main monitoring signal | data / concept drift | output quality, hallucination, cost, latency |
| Caching | usually not applicable | semantic caching pays off heavily |

The throughline: both treat the system, not the artifact, as the unit of engineering — but LLMOps moves the center of gravity from *training* to *serving and evaluation*, because the expensive, risky, fast-changing part is now what happens on every call rather than once per training run.

## 42.x — Key takeaways

- Operate the **lifecycle**, not the artifact: data → train → deploy → monitor → retrain is a loop, and MLOps automates it up a maturity ladder (manual → pipeline → CI/CD), adding **CT** (continuous training) to DevOps's CI/CD.
- **Reproducibility** needs three things together — seeds, pinned environments, and versioned data — plus experiment tracking; a metric you can't regenerate is worthless.
- Version **code (Git), data (DVC), and models (registry)** separately via **content-addressing**, and stitch them into **lineage** so any prediction traces back to its origins.
- **Orchestrators** (Airflow/Kubeflow/Prefect/Dagster) run training DAGs in batch; don't route live inference through them.
- **Feature stores** kill **training-serving skew** by computing each feature once and enforcing point-in-time correctness.
- Roll out with **shadow/canary/blue-green/A-B**; monitor for **data drift** ($P(x)$ moves) versus **concept drift** ($P(y\mid x)$ moves) alongside the operational **golden signals** (latency, errors, saturation) — concept drift is the one that quietly kills accuracy.
- **LLMOps** keeps the same skeleton but shifts the center of gravity to serving and evaluation: prompt versioning, **eval-in-CI** (with LLM-as-judge), token/cost tracking, semantic caching, tracing, and guardrails.

## 42.y — See also

- **Chapter 03 — The Machine Learning Process** — the lifecycle this chapter operationalizes.
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection** — the metrics and validation that gate deployment and eval-in-CI.
- **Chapter 30 — Large Language Models** — what LLMOps operates around, and where tokens are defined.
- **Chapter 31 — Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment** — prompts and adapters that LLMOps versions and evaluates.
- **Chapter 32 — Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search** — the retrieval and embedding stack behind semantic caching and traced chains.
- **Chapter 33 — AI Agents & Tool Use** — the multi-step chains that make tracing essential.
- **Chapter 43 — AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference** — the serving, batching, and hardware layer beneath deployment.
- **Chapter 37 — Anomaly Detection** — drift and outlier detection methods reused in monitoring.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 41 · 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics](ch41.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [43 · 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference →](ch43.html)
